title: Pearl Barley Risotto with Mushroom
subtitle: Orzotto-style: pearl barley cooked slowly in stock until creamy
excerpt: Pearl barley risotto uses pearl barley in place of Arborio rice, cooked in the same slow, stock-adding method as a conventional risotto. The barley takes longer to cook and never becomes quite as creamy as rice, but it has a pleasant chewiness and an earthy, nutty flavour that suits a mushroom base well. It is one of the more forgiving grain dishes: the barley is harder to overcook than rice.
sourceNotes: Pearl barley risotto (orzotto) is an Italian-origin technique adapted into British home cooking. Pearl barley has been a British staple grain since the medieval period. The orzotto method is widely documented in British food writing from the 2000s onward. Method adapted from standard orzotto technique with British ingredients.

glossaryTerms.definition: An Italian term for a dish made in the style of risotto but using orzo pasta or, in the British adaptation, pearl barley. The grain is stirred into hot stock added in stages, producing a creamy, porridge-like consistency. Pearl barley takes 40 to 45 minutes compared to 18 for Arborio rice.

content.text: is more forgiving than a rice risotto: the barley takes longer to become overcooked and can absorb stock over 40 to 45 minutes with only occasional stirring rather than the constant attention a rice risotto demands. Dried porcini mushrooms soaked in hot water add a deep, earthy base note; the soaking liquid makes excellent stock.

items.prepNote: soaked in 200 ml boiling water for 20 minutes, liquid reserved

content.text: Soften the base and toast the barley

Heat {{unsalted-butter}} of butter and {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large saucepan over a medium heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook gently for 8 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} and thyme and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{mushrooms-chestnut}} chestnut mushrooms and cook for 5 minutes until coloured and reduced. Add the soaked {{dried-porcini}} porcini (roughly chopped) and cook for 2 more minutes. Add {{pearl-barley}} of pearl barley and stir to coat in the fat. If using, pour in {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine and stir until absorbed.

Add the strained porcini soaking liquid to the {{stock-vegetable}} of hot vegetable stock. Add the combined stock to the barley one ladleful at a time, stirring and waiting until each addition is mostly absorbed before adding the next. Continue for 40 to 45 minutes, stirring regularly, until the barley is tender with a slight chew and the mixture is creamy. Stir in the {{parmesan}} Parmesan, season with {{sea-salt-flakes}} and {{black-pepper}}, and rest for 2 minutes before serving.

rows.cause: Insufficient stock or heat too low
rows.fix: Add more warm stock and continue cooking. Pearl barley can take up to 55 minutes depending on the age and type. Taste regularly from 40 minutes.

rows.cause: Too much stock absorbed without enough moisture remaining
rows.fix: Add a ladleful of hot water or stock and stir vigorously to loosen. The dish should be pourable, not stiff.

content.text: Pearl barley has been grown and eaten in Britain since ancient times; it was the standard grain of medieval British cookery before wheat flour became dominant. The orzotto method arrived from Italy via British restaurant cooking in the 1990s and 2000s and has become a standard in British vegetarian home cooking. It is a substantial autumn and winter dish, good on its own or alongside a simple salad.
